does: This paper introduces IBProtector, a defense mechanism that protects language models from jailbreaking attacks by applying information bottleneck principles to prompts. The method uses a lightweight, trainable extractor to selectively compress and perturb incoming prompts, retaining only task-relevant information while removing elements that might trigger harmful behaviors. The approach includes a modified objective to avoid trivial solutions and operates in both white-box and black-box settings where gradient information may be unavailable. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that IBProtector effectively mitigates various jailbreak attacks while maintaining response quality and computational efficiency.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research in adversarial robustness for neural networks and recent efforts to defend against prompt-based attacks on large language models. It extends the information bottleneck framework, originally developed for understanding information flow in deep learning, into the domain of adversarial defense. The paper responds directly to demonstrated vulnerabilities in aligned language models where carefully crafted or optimized adversarial prompts bypass safety training, addressing limitations of previous defense mechanisms that either significantly degrade performance or fail against diverse attack strategies. It combines insights from information theory with practical defense engineering to create a generalizable protective layer.
enables: This work enables deployment of more robust language models in high-stakes applications by providing a defense mechanism applicable to any target LLM without requiring retraining. The black-box capability particularly facilitates integration with proprietary or third-party models. Future research can extend this framework to defend against other prompt injection attacks, explore optimal information compression strategies for different model families, and investigate theoretical guarantees on robustness margins. The approach may inspire similar information-theoretic defenses for other modalities and opens questions about the fundamental trade-offs between safety, utility, and inference cost in large models.
